title: SEGMENT 11 — The resolution problem: voxels are bigger than cells

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — The resolution problem: voxels are bigger than cells
Here is the central technical mismatch of contemporary bioprinting, the one that the field is reckoning with even as the press releases get more breathless.
Cells are about 10 micrometers in diameter.
The smallest voxels — the smallest discrete units of printed material — that most extrusion bioprinters can produce are about 100 micrometers.
That's a factor of ten.
Your printing tool is ten times coarser than the biology you're trying to arrange.
Think about what that means in practice.

Imagine you want to print a piece of liver tissue with the proper lobular architecture, where the central vein is surrounded by radiating plates of hepatocytes one cell wide, with sinusoidal capillaries running between the plates.
A real hepatic plate is about 20 micrometers thick — two cells side by side.
The smallest feature you can print with a typical bioprinter is 100 micrometers — five cells thick.
You literally cannot resolve the architecture.
Whatever you print, the cells are going to be arranged on a much coarser grid than the natural tissue.
There are partial solutions to this problem.
Higher-resolution printing technologies — laser-induced forward transfer, two-photon stereolithography — can get down to single-cell resolution, but they're slow and limited in volume.
For a centimeter-scale construct, you might need tens of hours of printing, during which the cells are sitting in suboptimal conditions.
The trade-off between print resolution and print speed is real, and so far there's no technology that has both.
Another partial solution is to rely on self-organization within the printed material.

Even if you can't place cells with single-cell precision, you can place cell-laden hydrogel deposits in roughly the right places, and then let the cells inside those deposits sort themselves out using the same self-organization mechanisms that drive organoid formation.
We talked about organoids in another lecture.

The general principle is that cells have an enormous amount of architectural information encoded in their developmental programs, and given a permissive environment, they will reconstruct a great deal of the proper architecture on their own.
Bioprinting can be thought of as providing the coarse-grained structure — the macroscopic shape and the bulk cell positioning — while self-organization provides the fine-grained structure within.
The two together are much more powerful than either alone.
A third approach, and this is where the field has been moving rapidly, is post-print maturation in bioreactors.
You print a construct that is, at the moment of printing, an approximation.

You then put it in a bioreactor that applies mechanical, chemical, and electrical cues — the same kinds of cues a developing tissue would experience in vivo — and let the construct mature for days to weeks.
During that maturation, the cells remodel the printed structure, lay down new ECM, sort themselves into proper positions, and the architecture refines itself toward the target.
The printed object is a starting point, not a finished product.
The maturation step does the rest.

The general lesson here is that bioprinting, in its current state, is best understood not as a "print an organ" technology but as a "print a starting structure that will then self-assemble into an organ-like construct" technology.
The print sets the boundary conditions.
The biology fills in the details.
That distinction matters when you read press releases.
When a company announces it has "bioprinted a kidney," they have almost certainly not printed a kidney.
They have printed a kidney-shaped construct, with kidney cells in roughly the right places, that performs some kidney-like function in a dish.
The path from that print to a transplantable organ is long, and the bottleneck is rarely the printing itself.
It's the maturation, the integration, and especially — as we're about to see — the vascularization.
